items.title: Brute-force the single decision when the choice space is tiny
items.description: If the operation affects only one element and the number of possible choices is small, enumerating all choices is often simplest and safest.
items.content: When you must maximize/minimize an objective after changing exactly one position, iterate over all candidate positions, apply the change, compute the resulting score, and keep the best. This avoids relying on unproven heuristics and works especially well when constraints make recomputation cheap.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all use the same robust approach: because only one digit changes and each digit is small (bounded), the safest strategy is to try every possible choice of which position to increment, then compute the resulting product and take the maximum. The incorrect attempt failed because it applied an invalid global heuristic (“maximize the final product by incrementing the currently largest digit”), which ignores how multiplication couples all digits—especially when zeros are present or when multiple digits interact multiplicatively. Attempts that tried to “optimize” via division/zero counting either remained safe with careful zero handling or were effectively equivalent to brute force due to small constraints; the key difference is correctness over premature optimization.

items.title: Validate greedy heuristics against the true objective
items.description: Do not assume “increment the largest factor” optimizes a multiplicative objective, because interactions with other factors (including zeros) can flip the outcome.
items.content: A greedy rule based on local comparison may be tempting (e.g., choosing the largest digit to increment), but multiplication can make the global effect non-monotonic across positions. If you can construct counterexamples mentally (like presence of zeros or uneven factor sizes), that’s a sign to replace the heuristic with a correctness-guaranteed method.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all use the same robust approach: because only one digit changes and each digit is small (bounded), the safest strategy is to try every possible choice of which position to increment, then compute the resulting product and take the maximum. The incorrect attempt failed because it applied an invalid global heuristic (“maximize the final product by incrementing the currently largest digit”), which ignores how multiplication couples all digits—especially when zeros are present or when multiple digits interact multiplicatively. Attempts that tried to “optimize” via division/zero counting either remained safe with careful zero handling or were effectively equivalent to brute force due to small constraints; the key difference is correctness over premature optimization.

items.title: Handle special cases explicitly before using shortcut arithmetic
items.description: When using fast computations (like division or factoring), explicitly manage problematic cases such as zeros to prevent invalid or misleading results.
items.content: If an approach uses division to compute “product after change,” you must treat cases where the divisor might be zero separately. The general lesson is: any shortcut that changes algebraic form should be preceded by a check of the conditions under which it’s valid.

items.title: Greedy on earliest uncovered requirement
items.description: When operations can affect a limited contiguous window, an optimal strategy often starts each operation at (or relative to) the earliest remaining target that must be covered.
items.content: Scan from left to right; when you find the first still-unremoved “must-cover” element, take one operation that covers it as far to the right as allowed, then jump past the covered region. This avoids wasted operations and guarantees minimality because any feasible solution must spend at least one operation to cover the earliest uncovered requirement.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a consistent greedy/state approach: repeatedly choose an operation that removes an earliest uncovered black cell (or equivalently, scan left-to-right and whenever you encounter a black, count one operation and skip ahead by the operation length). Attempts that were labeled “partially correct” went astray by treating the problem as decomposable purely by maximal black segments and summing ceil(length/k) per segment without fully justifying overlap/interaction across positions; some of these also appeared to rely on an unproven independence assumption. Overall, the difference is that the correct solutions used a provably optimal “cover earliest uncovered” strategy (which implicitly handles how operations can overlap across whites), whereas failing attempts either used an incorrect greedy trigger (e.g., placing operations “at each found B” without considering coverage) or asserted segment independence without a rigorous argument for the given operation model.

items.title: Be cautious with “segment independence” assumptions
items.description: Do not decompose into independent blocks unless you can prove operations cannot couple them in a way that reduces total cost.
items.content: Maximal consecutive blocks are tempting to treat separately (e.g., summing ceil(length/k)), but this is only valid if operations cannot span across the separating structure in a way that changes the optimum. If the operation can overlap across boundaries, independence usually fails unless a rigorous argument shows it cannot improve or reduce the number of operations.

items.title: Coverage-based skipping beats local counting
items.description: Instead of counting based on local patterns, maintain the invariant of how far the current set of operations has “covered” the problem, and advance the pointer accordingly.
items.content: A correct algorithm keeps an implicit invariant such as “all black cells up to position i are already removable” and updates it in one step when you apply an operation. This yields both correctness (via invariants) and efficiency (by skipping entire windows that become irrelevant).
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a consistent greedy/state approach: repeatedly choose an operation that removes an earliest uncovered black cell (or equivalently, scan left-to-right and whenever you encounter a black, count one operation and skip ahead by the operation length). items.title: Validate transforms by mapping to the original constraint
items.description: Any reasoning that changes the problem view (e.g., turning it into per-block ceil calculations) should be checked against the original operation’s geometry.
items.content: If you derive a formula from a re-framing, test it conceptually against corner cases where operations could overlap across what you treated as boundaries. A reliable solution always has a direct argument that the derived cost matches the minimum number of operations under the original contiguous-window constraint.

items.title: Constraint-first: reduce to odd-frequency counting
items.description: For palindrome-rearrangement problems, reformulate the condition purely in terms of parity of character frequencies.
items.content: Before reasoning about deletions, translate “can be rearranged into a palindrome” into an exact constraint: for a multiset, it’s equivalent to having 0 odd counts when the length is even, and exactly 1 odd count when the length is odd. This reduction makes the problem depend on a single computed statistic rather than arrangement details.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converge on a consistent parity-and-reachability criterion: after deleting a fixed number of characters, only the number of odd-frequency letters in the remaining multiset matters (0 if remaining length is even, 1 if odd). Correct solutions also correctly account for how deletions can change that odd-count: each deletion flips parity of exactly one letter’s frequency, so the odd-count can move by 1 per deletion, and the remaining deletions beyond the minimum needed must satisfy a parity condition. Failures largely come from mixing up which “target” odd-count should be used, using incorrect formulas for the minimum deletions required, or skipping/loosening the parity constraint (often allowing negative diffs or incorrect assumptions about always being able to “increase” odds). Partially correct solutions sometimes get the parity constraint but mis-derive the feasibility range, or use incomplete/incorrect expressions for the minimum number of deletions to reach the target odd-count.

items.title: Track how operations change an invariant
items.description: When operations affect frequency parities, model the reachable states by how the key invariant changes per operation.
items.content: If each deletion flips the parity of exactly one letter count, then the number of odd-frequency letters changes by ±1 per deletion. This lets you compute feasibility by comparing the current odd-count to the required target odd-count after deletions and using the fact that you can only move the invariant stepwise with the available operations.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converge on a consistent parity-and-reachability criterion: after deleting a fixed number of characters, only the number of odd-frequency letters in the remaining multiset matters (0 if remaining length is even, 1 if odd). Correct solutions also correctly account for how deletions can change that odd-count: each deletion flips parity of exactly one letter’s frequency, so the odd-count can move by 1 per deletion, and the remaining deletions beyond the minimum needed must satisfy a parity condition. Failures largely come from mixing up which “target” odd-count should be used, using incorrect formulas for the minimum deletions required, or skipping/loosening the parity constraint (often allowing negative diffs or incorrect assumptions about always being able to “increase” odds). Partially correct solutions sometimes get the parity constraint but mis-derive the feasibility range, or use incomplete/incorrect expressions for the minimum number of deletions to reach the target odd-count.

items.title: Minimum reach + leftover parity check
items.description: Even when you can reach a target invariant value, an exact-operation requirement forces a parity condition on the remaining slack.
items.content: Compute the minimum number of deletions needed to get from the initial odd-count to the required target odd-count (as a distance). Then require that the remaining deletions can be “spent” without changing the invariant’s final value, which typically becomes a parity constraint like (k − minDeletions) being even.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converge on a consistent parity-and-reachability criterion: after deleting a fixed number of characters, only the number of odd-frequency letters in the remaining multiset matters (0 if remaining length is even, 1 if odd). Correct solutions also correctly account for how deletions can change that odd-count: each deletion flips parity of exactly one letter’s frequency, so the odd-count can move by 1 per deletion, and the remaining deletions beyond the minimum needed must satisfy a parity condition. Failures largely come from mixing up which “target” odd-count should be used, using incorrect formulas for the minimum deletions required, or skipping/loosening the parity constraint (often allowing negative diffs or incorrect assumptions about always being able to “increase” odds). Partially correct solutions sometimes get the parity constraint but mis-derive the feasibility range, or use incomplete/incorrect expressions for the minimum number of deletions to reach the target odd-count.

items.title: Handle negative differences explicitly (avoid mod-as-a-fix)
items.description: Do not rely on modulo/parity expressions to implicitly handle cases where a “difference” is negative; treat those cases with correct casework.
items.content: Several incorrect attempts implicitly allowed impossible transitions by using parity formulas on negative diffs or by assuming symmetry between increasing and decreasing the odd-count. A robust approach explicitly defines the target, computes distance to it, and applies reachability using inequalities plus a parity condition.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converge on a consistent parity-and-reachability criterion: after deleting a fixed number of characters, only the number of odd-frequency letters in the remaining multiset matters (0 if remaining length is even, 1 if odd). Partially correct solutions sometimes get the parity constraint but mis-derive the feasibility range, or use incomplete/incorrect expressions for the minimum number of deletions to reach the target odd-count.

items.title: Validate derived formulas against the invariants, not folklore
items.description: When using known contest “standard conditions,” verify each component (target choice, min deletions, parity slack) against the invariant model.
items.content: Wrong solutions often used partially correct “known” conditions but had inconsistencies (wrong target odd count or wrong min-deletions logic). Re-derive the condition from: (1) invariant definition, (2) exact per-operation change, (3) minimum steps to reach the required state, and (4) parity of leftover operations.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converge on a consistent parity-and-reachability criterion: after deleting a fixed number of characters, only the number of odd-frequency letters in the remaining multiset matters (0 if remaining length is even, 1 if odd). Failures largely come from mixing up which “target” odd-count should be used, using incorrect formulas for the minimum deletions required, or skipping/loosening the parity constraint (often allowing negative diffs or incorrect assumptions about always being able to “increase” odds). Partially correct solutions sometimes get the parity constraint but mis-derive the feasibility range, or use incomplete/incorrect expressions for the minimum number of deletions to reach the target odd-count.

items.title: Prime-Exponent Reformulation of Divisibility
items.description: Convert a product divisibility requirement into independent constraints on prime-exponent sums.
items.content: Factor k into prime powers and compute how many times each prime must appear in the product. Use v_p(a1·…·an)=Σ v_p(ai) and require the sum exponent to be at least the exponent in k. This invariant sharply narrows what “+1 operations” must accomplish.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a common correct modeling: express the divisibility condition via prime-exponent requirements of k, then compute the minimal number of “+1 to an element” operations to make the total prime exponents in the product meet those requirements. They also exploit the very small structure of k (≤5), which reduces the state space dramatically (often to a single prime with exponent requirement ≤2), allowing greedy or tiny DP. In contrast, unsuccessful attempts fail by (1) doing convoluted or incorrect DP/state encoding, (2) mixing “divisible by prime” intuition with “divisible product via exponent sums” without rigor, (3) using unsafe bounds for brute increments, or (4) introducing logical/implementation mistakes in transitions (wrong index math, placeholder/dummy values, or incorrect clamping/composition). Attempt 1 and 4 are close to correct but the unsafe/incorrect attempts reveal that getting transitions and capping right is critical; where solutions succeed, they keep the modeling aligned with the exponent-sum invariant and use a verified per-element “cost to reach exponent level” computed directly by simulating increments.

items.title: Min-Cost Per-Element to Hit Exponent Levels
items.description: For each element, precompute the minimal number of increments needed to make it contribute a certain capped prime exponent.
items.content: When you know the target exponent (often ≤2 for k≤5), enumerate small increments for an element and record the cheapest way to reach exponent ≥j (capped at the needed level). Then combine these per-element choices via greedy (when only one prime/level matters) or tiny DP (when you need exponent totals like two units). This keeps transitions correct and avoids guessing.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a common correct modeling: express the divisibility condition via prime-exponent requirements of k, then compute the minimal number of “+1 to an element” operations to make the total prime exponents in the product meet those requirements. They also exploit the very small structure of k (≤5), which reduces the state space dramatically (often to a single prime with exponent requirement ≤2), allowing greedy or tiny DP. In contrast, unsuccessful attempts fail by (1) doing convoluted or incorrect DP/state encoding, (2) mixing “divisible by prime” intuition with “divisible product via exponent sums” without rigor, (3) using unsafe bounds for brute increments, or (4) introducing logical/implementation mistakes in transitions (wrong index math, placeholder/dummy values, or incorrect clamping/composition). Attempt 1 and 4 are close to correct but the unsafe/incorrect attempts reveal that getting transitions and capping right is critical; where solutions succeed, they keep the modeling aligned with the exponent-sum invariant and use a verified per-element “cost to reach exponent level” computed directly by simulating increments.

items.title: Exploit Small k to Shrink State Space
items.description: Use the special structure of constraints (k≤5) to reduce complexity so DP/greedy becomes feasible and verifiable.
items.content: Because k has at most one relevant prime in this range (k=4 gives 2^2 with exponent requirement 2), you can collapse multi-dimensional exponent DP into a one-dimensional “need exponent units” problem. For cases requiring two units, only a small set of combinations must be checked (one element providing both units vs two elements providing one each). Always align the algorithm’s state size with the actual factorization complexity.

items.title: Validate State Encoding and Transitions
items.description: When using DP over multi-parameter states, ensure encoding/decoding and combination logic exactly match the mathematical definition.
items.content: Several failed attempts broke due to incorrect indexing, placeholder values, or wrong handling of caps/clamps during transition composition. A reliable approach is: define the mathematical state (exponent totals capped), compute next state using the same cap, and implement transition with direct arithmetic rather than opaque or error-prone encoding tricks. Add a sanity check by verifying base cases (already-satisfied → 0) and that DP never decreases contributions.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts share a common correct modeling: express the divisibility condition via prime-exponent requirements of k, then compute the minimal number of “+1 to an element” operations to make the total prime exponents in the product meet those requirements. Attempt 1 and 4 are close to correct but the unsafe/incorrect attempts reveal that getting transitions and capping right is critical; where solutions succeed, they keep the modeling aligned with the exponent-sum invariant and use a verified per-element “cost to reach exponent level” computed directly by simulating increments.

items.title: Use Safe Bounds for Brute Increments
items.description: When brute-forcing increments for “+1 operations,” choose an increment limit justified by the target exponent requirement.
items.content: Incorrect solutions often relied on ad-hoc bounds or assumed they were sufficient without tying them to how far an element must move to gain additional prime factors. Make the bound depend on the needed prime power: for v_p reaching at most 2, you only need to search until the next relevant multiples for that prime power are reachable (which is small under given constraints). If unsure, implement computation by checking divisibility directly rather than assuming a fixed small range.

<content>Factor k into prime powers and compute how many times each prime must appear in the product. Use v_p(a1·…·an)=Σ v_p(ai) and require the sum exponent to be at least the exponent in k. This invariant sharply narrows what “+1 operations” must accomplish.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Min-Cost Per-Element to Hit Exponent Levels</title>
<description>For each element, precompute the minimal number of increments needed to make it contribute a certain capped prime exponent.</description>
<content>When you know the target exponent (often ≤2 for k≤5), enumerate small increments for an element and record the cheapest way to reach exponent ≥j (capped at the needed level). Then combine these per-element choices via greedy (when only one prime/level matters) or tiny DP (when you need exponent totals like two units). This keeps transitions correct and avoids guessing.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Exploit Small k to Shrink State Space</title>
<description>Use the special structure of constraints (k≤5) to reduce complexity so DP/greedy becomes feasible and verifiable.</description>
<content>Because k has at most one relevant prime in this range (k=4 gives 2^2 with exponent requirement 2), you can collapse multi-dimensional exponent DP into a one-dimensional “need exponent units” problem. For cases requiring two units, only a small set of combinations must be checked (one element providing both units vs two elements providing one each). Always align the algorithm’s state size with the actual factorization complexity.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Validate State Encoding and Transitions</title>
<description>When using DP over multi-parameter states, ensure encoding/decoding and combination logic exactly match the mathematical definition.</description>
<content>Several failed attempts broke due to incorrect indexing, placeholder values, or wrong handling of caps/clamps during transition composition. A reliable approach is: define the mathematical state (exponent totals capped), compute next state using the same cap, and implement transition with direct arithmetic rather than opaque or error-prone encoding tricks. Add a sanity check by verifying base cases (already-satisfied → 0) and that DP never decreases contributions.</content>
</memory>

<memory>
<title>Use Safe Bounds for Brute Increments</title>
<description>When brute-forcing increments for “+1 operations,” choose an increment limit justified by the target exponent requirement.</description>
<content>Incorrect solutions often relied on ad-hoc bounds or assumed they were sufficient without tying them to how far an element must move to gain additional prime factors. Make the bound depend on the needed prime power: for v_p reaching at most 2, you only need to search until the next relevant multiples for that prime power are reachable (which is small under given constraints). If unsure, implement computation by checking divisibility directly rather than assuming a fixed small range.</content>
</memory>
</final>

items.title: Model as a turn-based reachability game (minimax), not a one-player distance problem
items.description: When an opponent can alter the state between your chances, you must analyze optimal play with minimax rather than using a “can I reach a target within k steps” heuristic.
items.content: Checking “is there a short path to a winning condition” often fails because the opponent changes the state after each move, potentially undoing your progress. Use game DP/minimax where each turn corresponds to state transitions and players have opposite objectives (maximize your win, minimize it).
items.reasoning: The “correct” solutions ultimately converge on a minimax/DP over a reduced state (move parity/turn and residue mod 3), while many “incorrect” attempts try to replace the game with a simplistic “distance to nearest multiple” or “n mod 3 decides” rule. The failures share a common pattern: they treat the check condition as if it were based only on the player who moves next, ignoring how the opponent’s moves change the residue before the next check opportunity. Another recurring issue is mishandling edge cases when the number is already divisible (the player must still move, and the check happens after that move), which breaks “immediate win” reasoning. The successful approach also reduces complexity by proving that only small information is needed (mod 3 and which player’s turn), making full minimax feasible under the small move limit.

items.title: Reduce the state to invariants relevant to the terminal condition
items.description: If the win condition depends only on a small invariant (e.g., divisibility), represent the game state using only that invariant plus whose turn/step count.
items.content: For games with ±1 moves and a divisibility-by-constant win condition, the full integer value is unnecessary; the state can be reduced to the residue class modulo that constant. This reduction makes exact minimax over the remaining limited horizon computationally practical and prevents incorrect overspecification.
items.reasoning: The “correct” solutions ultimately converge on a minimax/DP over a reduced state (move parity/turn and residue mod 3), while many “incorrect” attempts try to replace the game with a simplistic “distance to nearest multiple” or “n mod 3 decides” rule. The failures share a common pattern: they treat the check condition as if it were based only on the player who moves next, ignoring how the opponent’s moves change the residue before the next check opportunity. The successful approach also reduces complexity by proving that only small information is needed (mod 3 and which player’s turn), making full minimax feasible under the small move limit.

items.title: Validate special-case logic where “win after your move” changes what “already satisfied” means
items.description: When the win check occurs after a player’s move, being initially in a “winning-looking” state may not be winning; you must ensure a legal move can preserve or create the winning property at the checked time.
items.content: Many wrong attempts assumed that if the starting number is divisible, the current player wins immediately, but the rule required moving first and only then checking. Always align your reasoning with the exact timing of the terminal condition (after which player’s action and at what move count).
items.reasoning: The “correct” solutions ultimately converge on a minimax/DP over a reduced state (move parity/turn and residue mod 3), while many “incorrect” attempts try to replace the game with a simplistic “distance to nearest multiple” or “n mod 3 decides” rule. The failures share a common pattern: they treat the check condition as if it were based only on the player who moves next, ignoring how the opponent’s moves change the residue before the next check opportunity. Another recurring issue is mishandling edge cases when the number is already divisible (the player must still move, and the check happens after that move), which breaks “immediate win” reasoning. The successful approach also reduces complexity by proving that only small information is needed (mod 3 and which player’s turn), making full minimax feasible under the small move limit.

items.title: Use the move limit to bound depth and enable exact search
items.description: Small fixed horizons (like “after 10 moves”) allow exact minimax/DP that is safer than trying to derive a closed-form rule.
items.content: Instead of guessing a modular strategy, compute the outcome from all reachable states up to the terminal move count. This approach both resolves contradictions in sample-driven heuristics and guards against subtle parity/turn mistakes.
items.reasoning: The “correct” solutions ultimately converge on a minimax/DP over a reduced state (move parity/turn and residue mod 3), while many “incorrect” attempts try to replace the game with a simplistic “distance to nearest multiple” or “n mod 3 decides” rule. Another recurring issue is mishandling edge cases when the number is already divisible (the player must still move, and the check happens after that move), which breaks “immediate win” reasoning. The successful approach also reduces complexity by proving that only small information is needed (mod 3 and which player’s turn), making full minimax feasible under the small move limit.

items.title: Constraint-Driven DP State
items.description: Use a DP state that directly encodes the constraint locally (e.g., best valid subarray sum ending at each position).
items.content: For problems where validity depends on adjacency, define the state as “the best sum among all valid subarrays ending at index i”. Then the transition becomes simple: if the adjacency constraint is satisfied, you may extend; otherwise you must restart at i. This prevents accumulating sums that violate the rule.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all use the same robust DP invariant: track the maximum sum of a valid (alternating-parity) subarray that ends at the current index, updating it with either “extend” or “restart” depending on whether the parity alternates with the previous element. Failures in partially-correct attempts come from choosing an incorrect state (e.g., tying the validity to the starting element’s parity or accumulating without the correct restart rule), or from subtle misuse of parity/run logic that can allow invalid extensions. The correct versions also consistently include the base case and take the maximum over all “ending here” states.

items.title: Local Restart When Adjacency Fails
items.description: When a local constraint between neighbors is broken, the optimal solution must restart the segment at the current position.
items.content: If the alternating property fails between two consecutive items, any subarray that includes both cannot be valid, so the best “ending at i” candidate is forced to be the single element (or a fresh segment). Codify this as an explicit else-branch in the DP rather than trying to track higher-level run properties.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all use the same robust DP invariant: track the maximum sum of a valid (alternating-parity) subarray that ends at the current index, updating it with either “extend” or “restart” depending on whether the parity alternates with the previous element. Failures in partially-correct attempts come from choosing an incorrect state (e.g., tying the validity to the starting element’s parity or accumulating without the correct restart rule), or from subtle misuse of parity/run logic that can allow invalid extensions. The correct versions also consistently include the base case and take the maximum over all “ending here” states.

items.title: Max Over “Ending Here” States
items.description: Global maxima are obtained by taking the best value across all DP states, not just the final state.
items.content: Maintain both a running “best ending at i” value and an “overall best so far”. After each update, compare the current ending value to the overall answer. This avoids missing optima that occur before the last index.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all use the same robust DP invariant: track the maximum sum of a valid (alternating-parity) subarray that ends at the current index, updating it with either “extend” or “restart” depending on whether the parity alternates with the previous element. Failures in partially-correct attempts come from choosing an incorrect state (e.g., tying the validity to the starting element’s parity or accumulating without the correct restart rule), or from subtle misuse of parity/run logic that can allow invalid extensions. The correct versions also consistently include the base case and take the maximum over all “ending here” states.

items.title: Beware Over-Reduced State Anchors
items.description: Don’t anchor the logic to an indirect reference (like the first element’s parity) unless it’s proven sufficient for future validity.
items.content: Partially correct attempts that tracked parity relative to a starting element can silently allow invalid extensions because the constraint is determined by adjacency across the entire segment, not by a fixed global attribute. Ensure the state variable you keep is exactly what determines whether an extension step is allowed.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts all use the same robust DP invariant: track the maximum sum of a valid (alternating-parity) subarray that ends at the current index, updating it with either “extend” or “restart” depending on whether the parity alternates with the previous element. Failures in partially-correct attempts come from choosing an incorrect state (e.g., tying the validity to the starting element’s parity or accumulating without the correct restart rule), or from subtle misuse of parity/run logic that can allow invalid extensions. The correct versions also consistently include the base case and take the maximum over all “ending here” states.

items.title: Do algebra on the original expression, then verify the equivalence
items.description: When transforming exponentiation equations, intermediate “looks equivalent” steps can silently break the condition unless you check both directions against the original statement.
items.content: Several attempts made a plausible rewrite of the equality between exponential expressions into an equation on exponents, but then applied extra structure (e.g., “must be power-of-two ratio” or “monotonic implies only equality”) that wasn’t actually implied. A reliable approach is: (1) derive the exponent equation carefully, (2) test candidate characterizations by plugging back into the original equality, and (3) keep the characterization only if it works both ways.
items.reasoning: Most “correct” attempts were actually deriving/assuming an over-simplified characterization and then accidentally matching samples by also counting equal-exponent pairs. The successful submissions (Attempts 1/2/3/6 in the fully-correct section) share a robust pattern: reduce the condition to a small set of allowable relationships between the exponents, then count pairs by frequency and simple combinatorics. Failures come from (a) incorrect algebraic transformations that introduce wrong equivalences, and (b) trying to generalize an observed structure (like “only a special ratio works”) without validating it globally. Attempts that tried to compute a more complicated “normalized key” (odd part, v2-based keys, etc.) without a fully correct equivalence break—showing that choosing an invariant/key must be justified by rigorous implication in both directions.

items.title: Use a proven structural characterization to shrink counting to simple frequency sums
items.description: Many such problems become easy once you reduce the condition to “either always holds within a class or only holds for a small cross-class pattern,” allowing O(n) counting.
items.content: The accepted solutions effectively reduce all valid pairs to (i) pairs of equal parameters (always valid within that group) plus (ii) pairs across one narrowly defined pair of parameter values/classes. Once the valid relationship set is small, you can count with combinations like C(cnt,2) and products cnt[x]*cnt[y] rather than searching all pairs.
items.reasoning: Most “correct” attempts were actually deriving/assuming an over-simplified characterization and then accidentally matching samples by also counting equal-exponent pairs. The successful submissions (Attempts 1/2/3/6 in the fully-correct section) share a robust pattern: reduce the condition to a small set of allowable relationships between the exponents, then count pairs by frequency and simple combinatorics. Failures come from (a) incorrect algebraic transformations that introduce wrong equivalences, and (b) trying to generalize an observed structure (like “only a special ratio works”) without validating it globally. Attempts that tried to compute a more complicated “normalized key” (odd part, v2-based keys, etc.) without a fully correct equivalence break—showing that choosing an invariant/key must be justified by rigorous implication in both directions.

items.title: Prefer rigorously justified invariants/normal forms over heuristic “keys”
items.description: Normalization (odd part, v2 decomposition, gcd-based keys) is powerful only if the key is proven to be equivalent to the original condition, not just “consistent with intuition.”
items.content: Attempts that built complex keys like (odd part, derived exponent) or “same odd part” without a fully correct iff proof produced wrong counts. The transferable lesson: before adopting an invariant-based grouping, prove that equality of the key implies the original equality and vice versa; otherwise revert to a simpler characterization you can validate.
items.reasoning: Most “correct” attempts were actually deriving/assuming an over-simplified characterization and then accidentally matching samples by also counting equal-exponent pairs. The successful submissions (Attempts 1/2/3/6 in the fully-correct section) share a robust pattern: reduce the condition to a small set of allowable relationships between the exponents, then count pairs by frequency and simple combinatorics. Failures come from (a) incorrect algebraic transformations that introduce wrong equivalences, and (b) trying to generalize an observed structure (like “only a special ratio works”) without validating it globally. Attempts that tried to compute a more complicated “normalized key” (odd part, v2-based keys, etc.) without a fully correct equivalence break—showing that choosing an invariant/key must be justified by rigorous implication in both directions.

items.title: Count by frequencies after characterizing feasible relations
items.description: After determining which pairs are possible, compute the answer purely from counts—never by iterating over pairs—using combinatorics.
items.content: All successful approaches avoid O(n^2) by converting “valid pair patterns” into arithmetic over frequencies. This generalizes: once a problem’s condition depends only on category membership (or on membership in one/two categories), the solution is usually a small set of terms like sum over groups C(cnt,2) plus cross products.
